frontmatter.title: Independent sums in categories of monomorphisms

mainmatter.p: Independent sums play an important role in the semantics of local state, as developed by Kammar, Levy, Moss, and Staton and further refined by Polzer and Goncharov. Although these authors did not elucidate the universal property of independent sums, they alude to Alex Simpson’s notion of independence structure — which ought to provide, after taking opposite categories, a suitable abstract interface to the notion of independent coproduct. We will explore a special case of this abstract structure that is more concrete than the general case, but nonetheless allows for a conceptual reconstruction of the ideas of Kammar et al. based on simpler and more intuitive universal properties. Although it is important to be able to give explicit computations, a presentation that requires explicit computations does not scale to verifying the beautiful and intricate constructions of op. cit.

A co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} of monomorphisms in a category \mathcal {C} is called independent when its pullback is a span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookleftarrow \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } \hookrightarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} in which \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } is initial in \mathcal {C}.

The independent sum of a pair of objects u,v \in \mathcal {C} defined to be the initial independent co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}}, when it exists.

The independent complement of a monomorphism \rho : u \hookrightarrow v in a category \mathcal {C} is, if it exists, the greatest monomorphism v \ominus \rho \hookrightarrow v such that the co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ v \ominus \rho \hookrightarrow v \hookleftarrow u \right \} \mathclose {}} is an independent sum.
Question. Would it suffice to simply ask that the co-span is independent, but not universally so?
In an extensive category, any coproduct co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} is an independent sum.

As soon as the co-span { \mathopen {} \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} \mathclose {}} is independent, it evidently satisfies the universal property of independent sums, which is weaker than that of coproducts. Coproduct co-spans are always independent in an extensive category.
In an extensive category \mathcal {C}, the independent complement of a monomorphism is precisely the Heyting complement of that morphism when it exists.
This follows immediately from the fact that independent sums are coproducts in extensive \mathcal {C}.

Given a category \mathcal {C}, we define \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} to be the wide subcategory of \mathcal {C} spanned by monomorphisms. (As any isomorphism is a monomorphism, the obvious construction of the precategory \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} gives a category.)
Let \mathcal {C} be an extensive category. Then independent sums are created by the inclusion \mathbf {Inj} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {C} \right ) \mathclose {}} \hookrightarrow \mathcal {C}.

frontmatter.title: Category-theoretic structure for independence and conditional independence
frontmatter.meta: The Thirty-third Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXXIII)

@article{simpson-2018, author = {Alex Simpson}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2018.03.028}, issn = {1571-0661}, journal = {Electronic Notes in Theoretical Computer Science}, note = {The Thirty-third Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXXIII)}, pages = {281-297}, title = {Category-theoretic Structure for Independence and Conditional Independence}, volume = {336}, year = {2018}, }

mainmatter.p: Relations of independence and conditional independence arise in a variety of contexts. Stochastic independence and conditional independence are fundamental relations in probability theory and statistics. Analogous non-stochastic relations arise in database theory; in the setting of nominal sets (a semantic framework for modelling data with names); and in the modelling of concepts such as region disjointness for heap memory. In this paper, we identify unifying category-theoretic structure that encompasses these different forms of independence and conditional independence. The proposed structure supports the expected reasoning principles for notions of independence and conditional independence. We further identify associated notions of independent and local independent product, in which (conditional) independence is represented via a (fibred) monoidal structure, which is present in many examples.

frontmatter.title: A monad for full ground reference cells
frontmatter.meta: Proceedings of the 32nd Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

@inproceedings{kammar-levy-moss-staton-2017, author = {Kammar, Ohad and Levy, Paul B. and Moss, Sean K. and Staton, Sam}, address = {Reykjavik, Iceland}, publisher = {IEEE Press}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the 32nd Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science}, year = {2017}, month = jun, doi = {10.1109/LICS.2017.8005109}, isbn = {978-1-5090-3018-7}, title = {A Monad for Full Ground Reference Cells}, }

mainmatter.p: We present a denotational account of dynamic allocation of potentially cyclic memory cells using a monad on a functor category. We identify the collection of heaps as an object in a different functor category equipped with a monad for adding hiding/encapsulation capabilities to the heaps. We derive a monad for full ground references supporting effect masking by applying a state monad transformer to the encapsulation monad. To evaluate the monad, we present a denotational semantics for a call-by-value calculus with full ground references, and validate associated code transformations.

frontmatter.title: Local local reasoning: a BI-hyperdoctrine for full ground store
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{polzer-goncharov:2020, author = {Polzer, Miriam and Goncharov, Sergey}, editor = {Goubault-Larrecq, Jean and K\"{o}nig, Barbara}, address = {Cham}, publisher = {Springer International Publishing}, booktitle = {Foundations of Software Science and Computation Structures}, year = {2020}, isbn = {978-3-030-45231-5}, pages = {542--561}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2018.03.028}, title = {Local Local Reasoning: A BI-Hyperdoctrine for Full Ground Store}, }

mainmatter.p: Modelling and reasoning about dynamic memory allocation is one of the well-established strands of theoretical computer science, which is particularly well-known as a source of notorious challenges in semantics, reasoning, and proof theory. We capitalize on recent progress on categorical semantics of full ground store, in terms of a full ground store monad, to build a corresponding semantics of a higher order logic over the corresponding programs. Our main result is a construction of an (intuitionistic) BI-hyperdoctrine, which is arguably the semantic core of higher order logic over local store. Although we have made an extensive use of the existing generic tools, certain principled changes had to be made to enable the desired construction: while the original monad works over total heaps (to disable dangling pointers), our version involves partial heaps (heaplets) to enable compositional reasoning using separating conjunction. Another remarkable feature of our construction is that, in contrast to the existing generic approaches, our BI-algebra does not directly stem from an internal categorical partial commutative monoid.
